text: The surgeon inserts a calibrating bougie into the stomach, then identifies the stomach and begins to dissect the short gastric vessels connecting the stomach to the spleen along the greater curvature.
The surgeon uses traction, counter traction, and elevation to identify the tissue, then employs a robotic vessel sealer to divide the stomach from the short gastric vessels.

The surgeon uses the robotic vessel sealer to apply gentle pressure on the stomach, creating a small window near the spleen. The vessel sealer effectively identifies, seals, and transects vessels while cutting them.

The surgeon approaches the hiatus or gastroesophageal junction, where the top of the stomach connects to the esophagus, carefully freeing the area to ensure clear identification of the diaphragm muscles.
The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper in the right hand, performing the surgery through four small eight-millimeter incisions.
The surgeon separates the esophagus from the left crux of the diaphragm using a grasper to apply pressure in the opposite direction. The pars flaccida is entered, and the right crux is identified.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to open the pars flaccida, revealing the inferior vena cava (IVC) located to the left beneath the liver. The esophagus is being separated from the right crux of the diaphragm with counter traction applied to access the avascular plane.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the diaphragm muscles, taking care not to damage them, while preserving and identifying the patient's vagus nerve. The dissection connects points from the left side to the right side of the patient.

The surgeon enters the thoracic cavity, carefully identifying and freeing small perforators and vessels around the crux of the diaphragm. The gastroesophageal junction is visible, positioned at least four centimeters into the abdominal cavity, negating the need for a traditional three-sixty circumferential dissection.

The surgeon carefully identifies and separates tissue to free the esophagus, ensuring its mobility and confirming the connection between the stomach and esophagus is within the abdominal cavity. The surgeon preserves vessels and releases adhesions that may be pulling the hernia upwards.

The surgeon identifies the left and right crura of the diaphragm and frees the connections between the esophagus and diaphragm. The assistant places an Ethabon suture laparoscopically in preparation for closing the diaphragmatic hernia.

The surgeon uses a simple slip knot to approximate the muscles without strangulating them, carefully pulling and pushing down the suture. After a few throws, the surgeon uses a vessel sealer to cut the suture instead of having the assistant cut it, preparing to place the next suture.

The surgeon uses a simple slip knot technique, alternating throws to cinch the knot down, ensuring an adequate repair of the diaphragmatic hernia. A special sizing tube is visible in the patient's esophagus, guiding the tension of the repair to prevent discomfort or dysphagia during swallowing.

The surgeon prepares a window for the Nissen fundoplication, ensuring there are no adhesions or interference that could lead to ischemia. The anesthesiologist identifies and advances the tube in the patient's esophagus to aid in the procedure.
The surgeon uses a grasper to take down adhesions, creating a clear window for the Nissen fundoplication wrap. The window is checked to ensure it is not too tight, preventing potential ischemia.

The surgeon visually assesses the color of the liver, spleen, and surrounding areas. The fundus of the stomach is grasped and pulled through to create a 360-degree wrap, ensuring it is loose and floppy to avoid ischemia or stenosis. The stomach is identified and prepared for suturing to the esophagus.

The surgeon performs a shoe shine maneuver to ensure the stomach wrap is loose and mobile. Using a needle driver, the first suture is placed high, just below the gastroesophageal junction, securing a portion of the fundus on the patient's left side. A stabilizing suture is then placed through the esophagus, followed by a third suture on the right side of the stomach, completing the Nissen fundoplication wrap.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer as scissors to cinch down knots carefully, ensuring the sutures are snug but not too tight. The top of the fundoplication is started with alternating throws of Ethabon suture, and the vessel sealer is used to cut and remove any extra suture.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to perform another suture throw on the stomach, recreating the sphincter mechanism for the top of the Nissen fundoplication. The second throw is placed without passing through the esophagus, utilizing a slip knot technique.

The surgeon completes the suturing for the fundoplication, using a needle driver to snug down the knots. The Ethabon suture is used for its resilience and permanence. The vessel sealer is employed to cut and remove excess suture. The surgeon performs three throws, incorporating a healthy portion of the esophagus to prevent slippage of the hiatal hernia. The robotic system provides a clear, three-dimensional view of the anatomy.

The surgeon uses a thirty-degree scope pointed downward to visualize the Nissen fundoplication, ensuring the wrap is loose. A gastropaxi suture is placed at the top of the Nissen through the right crus of the diaphragm using a slip knot technique to prevent slippage. The vessel sealer is used to grab and remove the remaining suture. An injection of ICG dye is performed, and the Firefly technique is used to check for ischemia in the wrap and visualize the esophagus.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative endoscopy to evaluate the esophagus and the newly created fundoplication wrap. The endoscope is carefully inserted to assess the function of the hiatus and ensure the wrap is not too tight. Insufflation is used to open the top of the gastroesophageal junction, and the surgeon visualizes the stomach and repair, checking for healthy tissue color and perfusion.

The surgeon performs a visual inspection of the fundoplication wrap from the underside using eight millimeter ports. The assistant closes the skin incisions, while the surgeon ensures closure of any incisions larger than twelve millimeters and removes all gas from the patient.
